title: Day 1 · Phase 1 — Re-anchoring: the impact answer needs energy
bodyHtml:
DAY 1 · Phase 1 — From momentum to energy

Unit 3 said "big force." That's not yet a damage answer. Energy is.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS (preview)

K1. Work as a way energy moves. K2. Kinetic energy as the energy of motion. R4. Why 2026-XJ's KE is the load-bearing number for impact damage (preview).

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Where we are: end of Unit 3. We know 2026-XJ's momentum at impact. We know the average impact force is enormous. We don't yet have a damage estimate that matches what people actually use (Tunguska, Chicxulub) — those are reported in TNT equivalents.

Today's pivot: The number people quote for asteroid impacts is the asteroid's KINETIC ENERGY. Unit 4 builds that framework. Day 19 cashes it in.

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Return Unit 3 transfer tasks. Highlight one or two strong DART analyses. "You know momentum. Now: why isn't that enough?"
5–18 min

• Demo: drop a tennis ball from waist height onto a tile. Then drop the same ball from head height. Then drop a baseball from head height. Have students rank "how much did the floor feel that?" Push: what changed between cases? (Speed. Mass. Both at once.)
18–28 min

• Notice & Wonder. Push toward: there's a quantity that combines BOTH mass and speed in a NON-LINEAR way (energy is mv² not just mv). Don't formalize yet.
28–42 min

• Mini-lecture preview: Unit 4 is about energy. Three big payoffs: (1) cashing out the asteroid impact in TNT-equivalents (Day 19). (2) escape velocity — could 2026-XJ have ESCAPED if it had been faster? (Day 20). (3) why heat is missing energy from inelastic collisions (Day 18 / Unit 5 bridge).
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "If you double a moving car's SPEED, does the damage in a crash double, or more than double? Why might that be?" (Push toward the squared term — students don't need it formal yet.)
48–50 min

• Marzano on K1/K2/R4 preview.

Prep: Unit 3 transfer tasks scored; tennis ball; baseball; tile floor zone; whiteboard with a damage-ranking column.

Talk moves on the speed-vs-damage exit ticket: resist correcting. Let the room arrive at "speed matters more than mass" through the demo. The squared term will land cleanly on Day 4.

Differentiation: ELs — sentence frame "_____ damage more because _____"; SWDs — verbal version offered; advanced — "what NUMBER would you make to capture 'how hard to stop' AND 'how much damage'? Hint: not mass × velocity."

title: Day 2 · Phase 1 — Work: W = Fd
bodyHtml:
DAY 2 · Phase 1 — How energy moves

W = F·d. Units of Joules. Work is energy transferred by a force.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K1. Work definition. S1. Compute work in 1D.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Earth's gravity does WORK on 2026-XJ as it falls — that work increases the asteroid's energy. By the time it reaches Earth, all that work has accumulated into a colossal kinetic energy.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hand back Day 1 exit tickets. Anchor: "Today we name the quantity 'how much energy got moved.'"
5–15 min

• Direct instruction: W = F · d, where F is the force along the direction of motion. Units: Joules (N·m). Examples on the board: pushing a 100-N box 5 m → W = 500 J. Lifting a 50-N grocery bag 2 m → W = 100 J.
15–28 min

• Demonstrate ZERO work cases. Push a wall — F applied but d = 0, so W = 0. Carry a book level across the room — F is vertical (up), d is horizontal, no motion in F's direction, W = 0. (Tease tomorrow's angle case.)
28–40 min

• Group practice — 6 problems with varying F, d, and zero-work conditions. Force students to evaluate "is there motion IN THE DIRECTION OF THE FORCE?"
40–48 min

• Exit ticket: "You lift a 20-N book 1.5 m. How much work did you do? You then walk 3 m holding the book at that height. How much MORE work did you do?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K1/S1.

Prep: a book, a wall to push on, a small box, packet practice problems.

Talk moves on zero-work: when students say "I did work — I felt tired carrying the book" — push: "Did the book RISE while you walked? Was force aligned with motion? Where did your tiredness come from?" Distinguish biological energy from mechanical work.

Differentiation: ELs — visual showing F arrow + d arrow with alignment; SWDs — pre-printed evaluation checklist ("Is F nonzero? Is d nonzero? Is F along d?"); advanced — "if you carry the book UP the stairs, are you doing work? On what?"

title: Day 3 · Phase 1 — Work at an angle + negative work
bodyHtml:
DAY 3 · Phase 1 — Force not aligned with motion

W = F·d·cos θ. Friction does NEGATIVE work. Lifting against gravity does positive work.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K1. Work with angle. R1. Reason about sign of work.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If 2026-XJ enters the atmosphere, air friction does NEGATIVE work on it — taking energy away. (Hence: shooting stars glow as their KE converts to heat + light.)

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: W = F·d. Units?
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: when F is at angle θ to motion, only the COMPONENT of F along d does work. W = F·d·cos θ. When θ = 0 → W = F·d (full work). When θ = 90° → W = 0 (no work). When θ between 90° and 180° → W < 0 (negative work, energy taken AWAY).
18–32 min

• Worked example: pulling a 100-N suitcase 10 m at a 30° angle above horizontal. W = 100·10·cos30° = 866 J. Then: friction force 20 N opposing motion. W_friction = 20·10·cos180° = -200 J. Net work = 666 J.
32–42 min

• Group practice — 4 problems mixing angles and signs.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "You push a 30-N box 4 m across the floor at constant speed against 12 N of friction. What work did YOU do? What work did friction do? What's the NET work?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K1/R1.

Prep: Trig reference card with cos values for common angles; practice problems printed.

Talk moves on negative work: when a student says "friction does work" without the negative sign, stop. "In which direction is the friction force? In which direction is motion? What's the angle? What sign does that give?"

Differentiation: ELs — diagrams pre-drawn with F and d arrows + θ labeled; SWDs — column-based worksheet (F | d | θ | cos θ | W); advanced — "what's the net work on a satellite in CIRCULAR orbit over one revolution? Why?" (Zero — gravity perpendicular to motion. Preview Day 9.)

title: Day 4 · Phase 2 — Kinetic energy + work-energy theorem
bodyHtml:
DAY 4 · Phase 2 — KE = ½mv²; net work = ΔKE

The squared speed term lands today.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K2. Kinetic energy. K4. Work-energy theorem. S2. Compute KE.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The DAMAGE number for asteroids is their KINETIC ENERGY at impact. Today we get the formula, then build intuition for how big asteroid KEs are.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: revisit Day 1's question. "Doubling speed — does damage double?" Pose it again. Today we answer.
5–15 min

• Direct instruction: KE = ½mv². Walk through the formula. Units: Joules (kg·m²/s²). The SQUARED velocity is the load-bearing feature.
15–28 min

• Worked example sequence: KE of (a) a 1500-kg car at 30 m/s; (b) the SAME car at 60 m/s. Show that doubling v gives FOUR TIMES the KE. Repeat with (c) bullet, (d) sprinter, (e) 2026-XJ — same table as Unit 3 Day 2, now with KE instead of momentum.
28–40 min

• Work-energy theorem: the NET work done on an object equals its CHANGE in KE. W_net = ΔKE = KE_f − KE_i. Worked example: a 1-kg cart starts at rest. 10 N net force pushes it 5 m. What's its final speed?
40–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 2-kg ball at 3 m/s has 9 J of KE. If a net work of 27 J is done on it, what's its final speed?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K2/K4/S2.

Prep: KE-comparison table from Unit 3 Day 2 — refresh it with the KE column; calculator availability; the squared-v intuition demo ready (tennis ball at low + high speed).

Talk moves on KE quadrupling: when students are surprised, lean in. "This is why highway speeds aren't just twice as dangerous as 30 mph — they're FOUR times. Why?"

Differentiation: ELs — KE formula with each piece labeled in words; SWDs — column table for the worked examples; advanced — "if I'm a passenger going 60 mph and the car decelerates uniformly to 0 over 2 s, what was the work done on me by my seatbelt?"

title: Day 5 · Phase 2 — Work-energy theorem: worked examples
bodyHtml:
DAY 5 · Phase 2 — Hands on W_net = ΔKE

The work-energy theorem is a shortcut: skip kinematics, go straight from forces to final speed.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S4. Apply the work-energy theorem.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The asteroid's free fall toward Earth IS work done by gravity. The work-energy theorem lets us compute KE at impact from gravitational work — without ever solving for time or detailed velocity history.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick recap: W_net = ΔKE. Pull yesterday's example back up.
5–18 min

• Worked example 1: a 1500-kg car at 30 m/s brakes to rest. If the brakes apply 9000 N, how far does it travel? (Use W_net = -F·d = 0 − ½mv²; solve for d.)
18–32 min

• Worked example 2: a 100-kg roller-coaster car coasts down a 10-m incline. Net work done by gravity = mgh = 9800 J. What's its speed at the bottom? (Show this is FASTER than the kinematics route.)
32–42 min

• Group practice — 5 problems mixing braking, sliding, falling. Force students to identify NET work and apply ΔKE.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 0.5-kg ball is fired from a spring that does 8 J of work on it. The ball starts at rest. What's its speed?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on S4. Tomorrow: lab.

Prep: packet practice; calculator availability; KE/W reference card.

Talk moves on choosing the theorem: when students reach for kinematics on a complex case, push: "Do you know the FORCES? Do you know the DISTANCES? Could you just sum the work?"

Differentiation: ELs — worked example with each step in plain English; SWDs — fill-in-the-blank template (Step 1: identify W_net. Step 2: set = ΔKE. Step 3: solve for v_f.); advanced — "in the roller-coaster problem, friction takes 2000 J away. Now what's the bottom speed?"

title: Day 6 · Phase 2 — Vernier KE lab: measuring work-energy theorem
bodyHtml:
DAY 6 · Phase 2 — Empirical confirmation of W_net = ΔKE

Cart on a track, known applied force, measure final v with photogate, compare to predicted v.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S9. Vernier energy measurement. K4. Confirm work-energy theorem experimentally.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If W_net = ΔKE holds in our carts, we can TRUST it for any asteroid energy calculation we do later in the unit.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Lab overview + safety beat (level track, gentle pulls, photogates clamped).
5–18 min

• Teacher demo: cart on track. A known mass hanging over a pulley applies a measurable force. Photogate reads cart velocity at the end. Compute predicted v from W_net = ΔKE; compare to measured.
18–38 min

• Lab: pairs run 3 trials with different hanging masses. For each: compute W_net (force × distance), predict v_f, measure v_f, compute % difference.
38–45 min

• Class data compilation. Discuss sources of error: friction in pulley + track, mass measurement, photogate timing.
45–48 min

• Lab write-up start.
48–50 min

• Marzano on S9.

Prep: Vernier dynamics carts + tracks + pulleys + hanging masses; photogates + LabQuest; balance; lab write-up template.

Talk moves on % difference: if a group is at 12% off, push: "What in the setup did NEGATIVE work that we didn't count? Friction. Estimate it."

Differentiation: ELs — pair with a LoggerPro-fluent student; SWDs — pre-assigned roles per pair; advanced — "compute the friction force ON the track from your % difference. Is it reasonable?"

title: Day 7 · Phase 3 — Gravitational PE near Earth: U = mgh
bodyHtml:
DAY 7 · Phase 3 — Stored gravitational energy

U_g = mgh. The energy stored by lifting against gravity. Reference height matters.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K3. Gravitational PE near Earth's surface. S3. Compute PE.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

2026-XJ has gravitational PE relative to Earth. As it falls, that PE converts to KE. The PE is the "battery" the asteroid spends as it gets closer.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: "Where does a ball get its KE when it falls? It had energy BEFORE it fell. What kind?"
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: U_g = mgh (near Earth). Show the derivation: work done LIFTING a mass m up a height h against gravity = mg·h. That work is stored as PE.
18–28 min

• Reference height: PE depends on the CHOICE of zero. Choose ground = 0 → top has PE. Choose top = 0 → ground has -PE. Only the DIFFERENCE matters physically.
28–40 min

• Worked examples: PE of a 70-kg person at the top of a 100-m building (relative to street). PE of a 0.5-kg book on a 1.2-m table (relative to floor). PE of a 50-kg cart at the top of a 8-m ramp.
40–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 2-kg book on a desk 1.0 m above the floor. What's its PE relative to the floor? Relative to the desktop?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K3/S3.

Prep: packet practice; calculator availability; a tall object (broomstick) to show "height matters relative to what."

Talk moves on reference height: when a student insists their answer is "the right one," stop. "Both answers are right relative to a different reference. Which one matters depends on where the object will eventually FALL TO."

Differentiation: ELs — visual with PE values at multiple heights showing reference matters; SWDs — pre-printed problems with reference height stated explicitly; advanced — "if I'm on a tall building, is U = mgh accurate? When does it break? (When g changes with altitude — preview Day 9.)"

title: Day 8 · Phase 3 — When mgh fails: g changes with altitude
bodyHtml:
DAY 8 · Phase 3 — Why local PE breaks for asteroids

mgh assumes g is constant. For an asteroid 100,000 km out, g is NOT 9.8.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K3. Limits of mgh. Preview to universal PE.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If we tried to compute 2026-XJ's PE relative to Earth using mgh with g=9.81, we'd get a hilariously wrong answer. Tomorrow we get the right formula.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: U_g = mgh. Conditions?
5–18 min

• Probe: "What's the PE of a 1000-kg object at 100,000 km altitude using mgh?" Have students compute. Then: "What's g at 100,000 km altitude?" (From Unit 2: g = GM/r² → about 0.04 m/s².) The mgh formula assumed g = 9.81. The error is HUGE.
18–30 min

• Bridge: we need a PE formula that USES g(r). Set up: PE comes from integrating work done by gravity along the radial direction. Without calculus, just announce the result: U_g = -GMm/r.
30–42 min

• Discuss the negative sign: PE at INFINITY = 0; PE everywhere closer = negative. The lower the orbit, the MORE negative the PE. (Counter-intuitive but consistent — falling closer LOWERS PE.)
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "At Earth's surface, what's the PE of a 1-kg object using U = -GMm/r? Compare to U = mgh with h = 0." (Different references — that's OK. What matters is the CHANGE.)
48–50 min

• Marzano. Tomorrow: use the universal formula on the asteroid.

Prep: Unit 2's g(r) reference; calculator availability; equation cards (U = mgh AND U = -GMm/r).

Talk moves on the negative PE: students will hate this. Lean in. "At infinity, you have ZERO PE. As you fall toward a mass, your PE DECREASES (because gravity does positive work on you). Decreasing from zero gives negative."

Differentiation: ELs — sign chart (closer = more negative, farther = closer to zero); SWDs — pre-printed g(r) table for common altitudes; advanced — "derive U_g = -GMm/r by integrating F = -GMm/r² along r."

title: Day 9 · Phase 3 — Universal PE: U = -GMm/r and asteroid energy
bodyHtml:
DAY 9 · Phase 3 — Hands on -GMm/r

Computing the asteroid's PE at various distances. Where does its energy come from as it falls?

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K3. Universal PE. S3. Compute ΔU between altitudes.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Today: 2026-XJ's PE at its current distance. Its PE at impact. The DIFFERENCE is the KE it gains during the fall — that's the asteroid's KE at impact.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick recap of Day 8: U = -GMm/r. Negative sign convention.
5–18 min

• Worked example 1: PE of a 1-kg object at Earth's surface (r = 6.37×10⁶ m, M = 6×10²⁴ kg) using U = -GMm/r. Get the number.
18–32 min

• Worked example 2: 1000-kg asteroid at 384,000 km. PE = ? Asteroid at impact distance (r = 6.37×10⁶). PE = ? ΔU = U_final - U_initial. That's the KE it gained.
32–42 min

• Group practice — 4 problems: different masses, different distances. Each gets the ΔU as the asteroid falls.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 500-kg object falls from infinity (PE=0) to Earth's surface. What KE does it gain? (Ignore air friction.)"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K3/S3.

Prep: packet problems; calculator availability; G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹ reference; Unit 2 g(r) and r values.

Talk moves on infinity-to-surface ΔU: push: "This is the MAXIMUM KE a falling object can gain by Earth gravity. From infinity, with no other influences. What does this give a 1-kg object's speed AT IMPACT?" (Preview Day 20 escape velocity.)

Differentiation: ELs — equation card with each constant labeled; SWDs — pre-printed table for the ΔU calculation steps; advanced — "what's the impact speed of a meteorite falling from infinity?" (≈11.2 km/s — escape velocity.)

title: Day 10 · Phase 4 — Conservation of mechanical energy
bodyHtml:
DAY 10 · Phase 4 — KE + PE = constant

If only conservative forces act, mechanical energy is conserved.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K5. Conservation of mechanical energy + its condition.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

An asteroid in free fall (no friction) obeys ME conservation. The PE it loses as it falls = the KE it gains. By Day 19 we'll convert this to a TNT yield.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap: KE = ½mv². PE = mgh (near surface) or -GMm/r (general).
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: ME = KE + PE. When only gravity (or other conservative forces) act, ME stays constant. Friction is NOT conservative — it removes ME and turns it into heat. (Heat = Unit 5.)
18–32 min

• Worked example: ball dropped from 5 m. At top: KE=0, PE = 5mg. At ground: KE = ½mv², PE = 0. Set them equal: ½mv² = 5mg → v = √(2g·5) ≈ 9.9 m/s.
32–42 min

• Group practice — 4 problems mixing dropped-from-height, sliding-down-ramp, pendulum-swing. Each uses KE + PE = const.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 0.2-kg ball is dropped from 1.8 m. What's its speed at impact?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K5.

Prep: packet practice; pendulum bob + string demo prop; equation card (KE + PE = const).

Talk moves on the conservation rule: when students forget the friction caveat, stop. "What forces act on this object? Are ANY of them friction or air drag? If yes, ME is NOT exactly conserved."

Differentiation: ELs — before/after diagram with KE and PE values labeled at each; SWDs — fill-in template (KE_top + PE_top = KE_bot + PE_bot); advanced — "what happens to ME of a satellite in orbit? Why is it conserved?"

title: Day 11 · Phase 4 — Conservation: rolling, sliding, swinging
bodyHtml:
DAY 11 · Phase 4 — Multi-scenario conservation practice

Identify the energies. Apply KE + PE = const. Solve.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S5. Apply conservation of mechanical energy quantitatively.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Building the muscle memory we'll use on Day 19 for the asteroid impact yield.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: KE + PE = const. When?
5–20 min

• Worked example: 50-kg sled at top of 12-m hill, starts at rest. Friction-free hill. Speed at bottom? Then: same sled with KE 1000 J leftover going UP the next 5-m hill — what speed at top?
20–35 min

• Station rotation: 4 stations, 3-4 min each. (1) Pendulum: max height to bottom. (2) Roller coaster: height-to-speed-to-height. (3) Slide: ramp angle + length to bottom speed. (4) Falling: just PE to KE.
35–43 min

• Compile class answers. Pattern: technique is the SAME each time. "Identify high-PE and low-PE points, set ME_high = ME_low, solve."
43–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 0.3-kg pendulum bob is released from 0.6 m above its lowest point. Friction-free. What's its speed at the bottom?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on S5.

Prep: 4 station cards; calculator availability; equation reference card.

Talk moves on "why is technique the same?": after the station rotation, push: "What's invariant across all 4? Why doesn't the SHAPE of the path matter for the energy answer?" (Conservative forces.)

Differentiation: ELs — station cards with the conservation equation pre-written; SWDs — fewer stations, more time at each; advanced — "what's the speed at the BOTTOM of a 12-m hill if there's 200 J of friction? (Hint: ME isn't conserved.)"

title: Day 12 · Phase 4 — Roller coaster + pendulum: classic conservation problems
bodyHtml:
DAY 12 · Phase 4 — Two named scenarios

Roller coasters and pendulums are the textbook cases. Master them today; the Vernier lab tomorrow confirms.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S5. Apply conservation to roller coasters + pendulums.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

An asteroid in a closed elliptical orbit (like 2026-XJ around the Sun) IS a roller-coaster — periodic exchange between KE and PE. Faster at perihelion (low PE), slower at aphelion (high PE).

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: pendulum at the bottom — what energy form?
5–18 min

• Worked example: roller coaster with 3 hills (heights 30 m, 18 m, 22 m). Cart starts from rest at the top of hill 1. What's its speed at the top of hill 2? Top of hill 3? (Use ME conservation between any two points.)
18–32 min

• Worked example 2: 1-kg pendulum bob released from a height where it forms a 60° angle with vertical, string 1 m. Find speed at bottom of swing. (Height = L − L·cosθ = 1 − cos60° = 0.5 m.)
32–42 min

• Pattern-finding: orbital application. 2026-XJ at aphelion (slow, high PE) vs. perihelion (fast, low PE). The total ME is the same.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A roller-coaster cart starts at rest at the top of a 40-m hill. What's its speed at the bottom?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on S5.

Prep: packet practice; pendulum demo prop; roller-coaster sketch on the board.

Talk moves on the pendulum height computation: when students forget that h = L − L·cosθ, stop. "Draw the pendulum at angle θ. What height is the bob above its lowest point?"

Differentiation: ELs — pre-drawn diagrams with h labeled; SWDs — geometry helper sheet (h = L − L cos θ); advanced — "prove that the bottom-of-swing speed depends ONLY on the initial height, not on the string length."

title: Day 13 · Phase 4 — Vernier ramp lab: PE → KE conversion
bodyHtml:
DAY 13 · Phase 4 — Empirical conservation

Cart rolling down an incline. Vernier reads v at the bottom. Compare predicted v from mgh = ½mv² to measured.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S9. Vernier measurement. K5. Conservation of ME, experimental confirmation.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If PE-to-KE conversion holds in our cart, we can TRUST the same logic when we compute asteroid impact KE on Day 19.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Lab overview + safety beat.
5–18 min

• Teacher demo: cart on incline (say 15°), released from rest at a known height. Photogate at the bottom reads v. Compute v from PE = KE → v = √(2gh). Compare.
18–38 min

• Lab: pairs run 3 trials at DIFFERENT release heights (10 cm, 20 cm, 30 cm). For each: predict v from mgh = ½mv², measure v, compute % difference.
38–45 min

• Class data: plot measured v² vs. h. Should be linear with slope 2g. (Or compute g from the data!)
45–48 min

• Lab write-up start.
48–50 min

• Marzano on S9.

Prep: dynamics carts + tracks + photogates; balance; protractor/clinometer for incline angle; lab write-up template.

Talk moves on the v² vs. h plot: after students plot, ask "What's the slope? What's 2g? Does that match?" The lab measures g indirectly.

Differentiation: ELs — pair with LoggerPro-fluent student; SWDs — pre-built data table; advanced — "compute the friction force in the cart by accounting for the energy deficit."

title: Day 14 · Phase 5 — Hooke's law: springs
bodyHtml:
DAY 14 · Phase 5 — Springs as energy storers

F = -kx. Springs apply force PROPORTIONAL to displacement.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K7. Hooke's law. S7. Compute spring force for given compression/extension.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Spring physics is the template for ELASTIC restoring forces — useful both for crumple-zone modeling on Day 18 (energy dissipation) and for understanding shock during impact.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: stretch a spring with your hand. The further you pull, the harder it pulls back. Why?
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: F_spring = -kx, where k is the spring constant (N/m) and x is displacement from equilibrium. Negative sign: restoring (always pulls back to equilibrium).
18–32 min

• Demo: hang masses from a spring, measure displacement. Plot F vs. x. Should be linear with slope k. (Or do this as the lab — see optional flex.)
32–42 min

• Worked example: a spring stretches 5 cm under a 10-N load. What's k? If the same spring is stretched 12 cm, what force does it exert?
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A spring with k = 200 N/m is compressed 0.08 m. What force does it exert?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K7/S7.

Prep: spring + hanging masses + meter stick demo; F-vs-x graph paper; calculator availability.

Talk moves on the negative sign: when students forget it, push: "What direction is the force? What direction is the displacement? Are they the same? What does that tell you about the sign?"

Differentiation: ELs — sign convention chart (compressed/stretched/force direction); SWDs — pre-built F-vs-x table with k computation; advanced — "derive the spring constant for two springs in series. In parallel."

title: Day 15 · Phase 5 — Spring potential energy: ½kx²
bodyHtml:
DAY 15 · Phase 5 — Energy stored in springs

U_spring = ½kx². The energy you put in by compressing.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K7. Spring PE formula. S7. Compute spring PE; apply to launch problems.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If we had a SPRING-based launcher to deflect 2026-XJ, the energy delivered would be ½kx². Compare to KE = ½mv² — they have the SAME structure. (Squared variable times a constant.)

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: Hooke's law. F = -kx.
5–15 min

• Derivation move: U = area under F-vs-x graph (triangle). Area = ½ · x · F_max = ½ · x · kx = ½kx².
15–28 min

• Worked example: a spring with k = 500 N/m is compressed 0.20 m. How much PE is stored? If a 0.4-kg ball is launched off this spring (frictionless, horizontal), what's its speed?
28–40 min

• Group practice — 4 problems mixing spring + cart/ball/projectile. Each uses ½kx² = ½mv² (or = mgh for vertical launches).
40–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A spring (k = 800 N/m) is compressed 5 cm. It launches a 0.1-kg ball straight up. How high does the ball go (friction-free)?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K7/S7.

Prep: spring + launching gun toy if available; packet practice; F-vs-x area visualization.

Talk moves on PE = ½kx²: when students confuse this with W = Fx, push: "Is the force CONSTANT across the compression? No — it grows linearly. Average force is ½ kx, distance is x. Energy = avg force × distance = ½kx²."

Differentiation: ELs — pre-drawn F-vs-x triangle with area shaded; SWDs — fill-in-the-blank conservation equation (½kx² = ½mv² or = mgh); advanced — "if friction takes 20% of the energy, how high does the ball go now?"

title: Day 16 · Phase 5 — Power: how fast work gets done
bodyHtml:
DAY 16 · Phase 5 — P = W/t

Power tells you the RATE of energy transfer. Watts = Joules per second.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K6. Power definition. S6. Compute power.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Asteroid impact delivers HUGE energy in MICROSECONDS. The POWER is what makes impacts catastrophic — it's not just the energy, it's how concentrated the delivery is in time.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: "You climb a flight of stairs. Slow or fast — same work done. What's different?"
5–15 min

• Direct instruction: P = W/t. Units: Watts (J/s). Also: P = F·v (force times velocity, instantaneous case).
15–28 min

• Worked examples: a 700-N student climbs a 4-m staircase in 5 s. P = 700·4/5 = 560 W. Compare to a 100-W lightbulb. A car engine outputs 100 hp (≈75 kW) at 60 mph (27 m/s) — how much driving force?
28–40 min

• Group practice — 4 problems. One: "How much energy does a 60-W bulb use in 1 hour? In Joules." One: "A weightlifter does 800 J of work in 0.4 s. Power?"
40–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 2-kg ball is lifted 1.5 m in 0.3 s. What power did the lifter generate?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K6/S6.

Prep: staircase reference (count steps); a watt-hour meter or lightbulb prop; packet practice.

Talk moves on P = F·v: derive it from P = W/t. "W = F·d. So P = F·d/t. What IS d/t? That's velocity."

Differentiation: ELs — power-units conversion chart (J/s = W; kWh as a familiar unit); SWDs — pre-built unit-conversion ladder; advanced — "a car needs 50 kW to maintain 30 m/s on a flat road. What's the air-drag force?"

title: Day 17 · Phase 6 — KE in collisions revisited: elastic vs. inelastic
bodyHtml:
DAY 17 · Phase 6 — Quantitative energy in collisions

Compute KE_before vs KE_after for elastic + inelastic. Where does the missing energy go?

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R2. Track energy transformations through collisions.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Asteroid impact = perfectly inelastic. The asteroid's KE doesn't disappear — it converts to heat, sound, mechanical deformation, light. THAT'S the damage.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: which collision conserves both p and KE? (Elastic.) Which conserves only p? (Inelastic.)
5–18 min

• Worked example: 1-kg ball at 6 m/s strikes a 1-kg ball at rest. Compute KE_before. (a) ELASTIC: KE_after using Unit 3 Day 9 result (v₁f=0, v₂f=6). KE_after same as before. (b) PERFECTLY INELASTIC: stick together, v_f = 3 m/s (Unit 3 Day 8). KE_after = ½·2·9 = 9 J. KE_before was ½·1·36 = 18 J. HALF the energy is GONE.
18–32 min

• Where did the missing 9 J go? Lead the class to: heat, deformation, sound. The pieces stuck together — energy was spent making them stick.
32–42 min

• Group practice — 3 collision problems. Each: compute KE_before, KE_after, ΔKE. Inelastic always loses energy.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 0.5-kg ball at 10 m/s strikes a 1.5-kg stationary block; they stick. How much KE was lost?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on R2. Tomorrow: HEAT is the missing energy.

Prep: packet practice; calculator; Unit 3 collision-type reference card.

Talk moves on "energy is gone": when students say "energy disappeared," stop. "Energy doesn't DISAPPEAR. It CHANGES FORM. Into what?"

Differentiation: ELs — before/after KE table; SWDs — pre-printed (KE_before − KE_after = ΔKE_lost); advanced — "derive a formula for the fraction of KE lost in a perfectly inelastic head-on collision between two masses."

title: Day 18 · Phase 6 — Heat as the missing energy (Unit 5 preview)
bodyHtml:
DAY 18 · Phase 6 — Conservation of ENERGY (broader than ME)

Friction + inelastic collisions transfer ME to thermal energy. Total energy is always conserved.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K8. Thermal energy as the destination for "lost" ME. K5. Conservation of TOTAL energy.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

2026-XJ's KE at impact becomes HEAT. Enough heat to vaporize rock, raise tsunami, ignite forests. Unit 5 tells us how to quantify the heat itself; today's our bridge.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Recap Day 17: KE was "lost" in inelastic collisions. Where did it go?
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: thermal energy is the energy of random molecular motion. ME lost to friction or inelastic collision becomes thermal — the objects get WARMER (and the surroundings).
18–30 min

• Demo: rub your hands together. They warm up. Mechanical work → thermal energy. Quantify: 100 N of friction over 0.5 m of relative motion = 50 J of thermal energy.
30–42 min

• Broader conservation principle: total energy (KE + PE + thermal + radiant + sound + ...) is ALWAYS conserved. ME only conservation is a special case (no friction).
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 50-kg sled slides 12 m down a snowy hill (8 m drop) and stops at the bottom. How much heat was generated by friction?" (Hint: PE_top = thermal generated.)
48–50 min

• Marzano on K8/K5. Unit 5 picks up here.

Prep: rub-your-hands demo prompt; packet practice; energy-conservation flowchart for the board.

Talk moves on the sled problem: when students struggle, push: "The sled STARTED with PE. It ENDED at rest with KE = 0 and PE = 0. Where did the energy go?"

Differentiation: ELs — energy-conservation flow diagram with arrows from ME to thermal; SWDs — pre-printed conservation equation (PE_initial = thermal_generated for stopped objects); advanced — "estimate the temperature rise of a steel block subjected to 1000 J of friction. Hint: specific heat of steel is 450 J/(kg·°C)."

title: Day 19 · Phase 7 — Asteroid impact yield: KE in TNT equivalents
bodyHtml:
DAY 19 · Phase 7 — The unit's payoff calculation

Compute 2026-XJ's KE at impact. Convert to TNT yield. Compare to known events.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R4. Apply energy framework to predict asteroid impact effects.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Today the energy framework cashes out. Students compute the asteroid's KE at impact and express it as a TNT yield (1 ton TNT ≈ 4.2×10⁹ J). Compare to: Hiroshima (15 kilotons), Tunguska (15 megatons), Chicxulub (100 million megatons).

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Frame: "Today we finally answer the impact question with a real number."
5–15 min

• Given 2026-XJ's parameters (mass, impact velocity from Unit 2). Compute KE = ½mv². Get the number in Joules.
15–28 min

• Convert to TNT: 1 ton TNT = 4.184×10⁹ J. So 2026-XJ's KE in TNT-equivalent tons = KE/(4.2×10⁹).
28–40 min

• Comparison table on the board: Hiroshima 1.5×10⁴ tons. Tunguska 1.5×10⁷ tons. Chelyabinsk 5×10⁵ tons. Chicxulub 10¹⁴ tons. Where does 2026-XJ fall on this scale?
40–48 min

• Discussion: what does this mean PHYSICALLY for Earth? Local destruction? Regional? Civilization-ending? Each tier connects to a yield range.
48–50 min

• Marzano on R4. Tomorrow: escape velocity.

Prep: 2026-XJ parameter card; calculator; TNT-equivalent conversion sheet; comparison table pre-drawn on the board.

Talk moves on the yield comparison: when students reach for civilizational stakes, lean in carefully. "The math is real. The asteroid is fictional. But this is the framework astronomers actually use."

Differentiation: ELs — TNT conversion sheet pre-printed with each comparison; SWDs — calculator with the unit conversion pre-set; advanced — "if 2026-XJ's mass were OFF by a factor of 2, how does the yield change?"

title: Day 20 · Phase 7 — Escape velocity
bodyHtml:
DAY 20 · Phase 7 — How fast to escape Earth's gravity

v_esc = √(2GM/r). Set KE = |U| and solve.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R5. Reason about escape velocity using conservation of energy. S8. Compute escape velocity.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If 2026-XJ had been MOVING FASTER, it could have escaped Earth's gravity well entirely — no impact, no orbit, just fly past. The threshold is escape velocity.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: "What's the SLOWEST speed at which you can throw a baseball straight up and NEVER have it come back?"
5–18 min

• Derivation: object at Earth's surface with KE = ½mv² and PE = -GMm/r. For escape, total energy must be ≥ 0 (so it reaches infinity with at least 0 KE). Set ½mv_esc² = GMm/r. Solve: v_esc = √(2GM/r).
18–30 min

• Compute Earth's escape velocity: v_esc = √(2·6.67×10⁻¹¹·6×10²⁴/6.37×10⁶) ≈ 11.2 km/s. That's 25,000 mph.
30–42 min

• Worked examples: escape velocity from Moon (much smaller). From Mars. From a 1000-km asteroid (tiny — almost nothing). What does this tell us about why small bodies don't hold atmospheres?
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "If you were on Mars, what's the escape velocity? (M_Mars = 6.4×10²³ kg, r_Mars = 3.39×10⁶ m.)"
48–50 min

• Marzano on R5/S8.

Prep: equation cards (v_esc and v_orbit from Unit 2 Day 13); calculator; planet parameter sheet (M and r for Moon, Mars, asteroid).

Talk moves on "why bodies hold atmospheres": push: "What's the SPEED of an air molecule at room temperature? If it's BELOW escape velocity, atmosphere stays. ABOVE, atmosphere drifts away. Compare for Earth vs. Moon."

Differentiation: ELs — v_esc formula with each piece labeled; SWDs — calculator with constants pre-loaded; advanced — "derive: v_esc = √2 · v_orbit. Why?"

title: Day 21 · Phase 8 — Putting it together: 2026-XJ energy budget
bodyHtml:
DAY 21 · Phase 8 — The unit's payoff day

Compose all of Unit 4 into one full asteroid energy analysis.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

All Unit 4 targets surfaced through one complete analysis.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Three branches to analyze: (1) asteroid impacts, what's the energy yield? (2) asteroid is deflected (Unit 3 DART work), what's its NEW orbital energy? (3) asteroid escapes Earth — was its current speed below or above escape from the Sun?

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Frame the day. "Three energy branches. We compute all three."
5–18 min

• Group station 1 — IMPACT YIELD. Using Day 19 framework, compute 2026-XJ's KE at impact and TNT equivalent. Quick recap.
18–32 min

• Group station 2 — DEFLECTION ENERGY. Given a DART-style spacecraft delivering Δv (from Unit 3), what KE does THAT require? Is it feasible with current rocket tech?
32–42 min

• Group station 3 — ESCAPE. Using v_esc from the Sun at Earth's orbit (≈42 km/s), is 2026-XJ's current velocity above or below? If below, it CANNOT escape the solar system without external boost.
42–48 min

• Whole-class compilation. Build the energy budget. Tomorrow: transfer task.
48–50 min

• Marzano on full unit target list.

Prep: 2026-XJ parameter card; v_esc reference sheet (Earth + Sun); calculator availability; whiteboard space for compilation.

Talk moves on the deflection energy: push: "Δv times mass gives momentum delivered. KE delivered is ½m·Δv². Which is harder to GET to the asteroid?" (Both — but energy puts a hard limit on rocket feasibility.)

Differentiation: ELs — three-column table (impact / deflection / escape); SWDs — station role-cards; advanced — "propose ONE planetary defense strategy that maximizes ENERGY DELIVERY EFFICIENCY."

title: Day 22 · Phase 8 — Unit 4 transfer task
bodyHtml:
DAY 22 · Phase 8 — Assessment of learning

5 multi-part problems integrating work, KE, PE, conservation, springs, power, escape velocity.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

All Unit 4 targets, surfaced through transfer problems.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The transfer task includes an asteroid impact-yield problem (R4) and an escape-velocity problem (R5/S8) as the load-bearing Reasoning prompts.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Brief reminder of the 4-dimension rubric (Identify / Calculate / Construct / Transfer). Lowest-dimension grading.
5–45 min

• Transfer task. Independent. 5 multi-part problems: (1) work + work-energy theorem on a braking car; (2) roller-coaster speed at multiple points using ME conservation; (3) spring-launched projectile using ½kx² → ½mv² + mgh; (4) compute power required by an elevator; (5) asteroid impact yield IN TONS TNT + escape velocity from a given body.
45–48 min

• Final Marzano on the unit target list.
48–50 min

• Growth chart updated. Unit 5 begins tomorrow — thermal physics picks up where today's heat preview left off.

Prep: transfer task printed; calculators; constants reference card (G, M_Earth, r_Earth, J-per-ton-TNT); quiet testing environment; growth chart pages.

Differentiation on the transfer task: ELs — vocabulary glossary attached (work, energy, conservation, escape velocity, yield); SWDs — option to verbal-explain dimension 4 (Transfer) of any problem; advanced — bonus prompt: "Use Unit 4 thinking to estimate the SMALLEST asteroid that could deliver a Hiroshima-equivalent impact at typical asteroid speeds."

Closing: Growth chart picked up tomorrow at start of Unit 5 — the energy that was "lost to heat" gets its own framework.
